explanation: The National Human Rights Commission submits its annual or special reports to the Central Government and to the concerned State Governments. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect. UPSC, National Commission of ST and Central Vigilance Commission present their annual report of performance to the President, who puts it in front of each house of Parliament. Hence, Statement 2, 3 and 4 is correct.
subject: Indian Polity
difficulty_rationale: Tests factual knowledge of the reporting mechanisms of various constitutional and statutory bodies.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I
Only Union Home Secretary can pass order for suspension of any telegraph service (including internet)
Statement-II
Posts and telegraphs and other forms of communication comes under Union List
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: a) Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I
b) Both Statement-I and Statement- II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
c) Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
d) Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct
explanation: Section 5(2) of the Indian Telegraph Act, 1885, in conjunction with the Temporary Suspension of Telecom Services (Public Emergency and Public Safety) Rules, 2017, empowers the Union or State Home Secretary to authorize the suspension of any telegraph service, including the internet, in the event of a public emergency or for reasons of public safety.These rules allow the union or state home secretary to order the suspension of any telegraph service (including internet) in case of public emergency or public safety. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect. Entry no 31 in Union List “Posts and telegraphs; telephones, wireless, broadcasting and other like forms of communication”. Hence, Statement 2 is correct.
subject: Indian Polity
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of specific statutory provisions regarding telecom suspension and the Union List entries.

questionText:
Consider the following Statements:
1. Reservation of a bill for consideration of the President.
2. Appointment of Chief Minister when no party has a clear majority.
3. Dissolution of assembly if the council of ministers has lost its majority.
How many of the above are considered as Constitutional Discretion of Governor?
explanation: The governor has constitutional discretion in the following cases: Reservation of a bill for the consideration of the President. Hence, Statement 1 is correct. Recommendation for the imposition of the President’s Rule in the state. While exercising his functions as the administrator of an adjoining union territory (in case of additional charge). Determining the amount payable by the Government of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram to an autonomous Tribal District Council as royalty accruing from licenses for mineral exploration. Seeking information from the chief minister with regard to the administrative and legislative matters of the state. In addition to the above constitutional discretion (i.e., the express discretion mentioned in the Constitution), the governor, like the president, also has situational discretion (i.e., the hidden discretion derived from the exigencies of a prevailing political situation) in the following cases: Appointment of chief minister when no party has a clear-cut majority in the state legislative assembly or when the chief minister in office dies suddenly and there is no obvious successor. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect. Dismissal of the council of ministers when it cannot prove the confidence of the state legislative assembly. Dissolution of the state legislative assembly if the council of ministers have lost its majority. Hence, Statement 3 is incorrect.
subject: Indian Polity
difficulty_rationale: Tests the distinction between constitutional discretion and situational discretion of the Governor.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. Unlike the Prime Minister of Britain, the Prime Minister of India can be a member of any of the two Houses of Parliament.
2. Like in Britain, the Union Ministers in India are required to countersign Acts assented by the President of India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Parliamentary System of Government in India is largely based on the British Parliamentary System. However, there are some differences between the two: In Britain, the Prime Minister should be a member of the Lower House (House of Commons) of the Parliament. In India, the Prime Minister may be a member of any of the two Houses of Parliament. So, statement 1 is correct. Britain has the system of legal responsibility of the Minister while India has no such system. Unlike in Britain, the ministers in India are not required to countersign the official acts of the Head of the State. So, statement 2 is not correct.
subject: Indian Polity
difficulty_rationale: Requires comparative knowledge of the Indian and British parliamentary systems.

questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding Attorney General of India:
1. He/She is the highest law officer in the country.
2. He/She is appointed by Chief Justice of India
3. He/She can be a member of a Parliamentary committee with right to vote.
4. He/She enjoys all privileges and immunity that are available to Members of Parliament.
How many of the above Statements are incorrect?
explanation: The Constitution (Article 76) has provided for the office of the Attorney General for India. He is the highest law officer in the country. Hence, Statement 1 is correct. The Attorney General (AG) is appointed by the president. He must be a person who is qualified to be appointed a judge of the Supreme Court. In other words, he must be a citizen of India and he must have been a judge of some high court for five years or an advocate of some high court for ten years or an eminent jurist, in the opinion of the president. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect. Attorney General has the right of audience in all courts in the territory of India. Further, he has the right to speak and to take part in the proceedings of both the Houses of Parliament or their joint sitting and any committee of the Parliament of which he may be named a member, but without a right to vote. Hence, Statement 3 is incorrect. He enjoys all the privileges and immunities that are available to a member of Parliament. Hence, Statement 4 is correct.
subject: Indian Polity
difficulty_rationale: Tests detailed knowledge of the qualifications, appointment, and rights of the Attorney General of India.
